name: Hatshepsut
description: Hatshepsut was the fifth pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt. She is generally regarded by Egyptologists as one of the most successful pharaohs, reigning longer than any other woman pharaoh. Hatshepsut was the daughter of Thutmose I and Ahmose. She became queen of Egypt when she married her half-brother, Thutmose II. After his death, she served as regent for her stepson, Thutmose III, who was too young to rule. She later declared herself pharaoh, adopting the full regalia and titles traditionally associated with male rulers. Her reign was marked by peace, prosperity, and ambitious building projects, including the magnificent mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri. She promoted trade expeditions, most notably to the Land of Punt, which brought back exotic goods and strengthened Egypt's economy. Hatshepsut's legacy is complex. After her death, Thutmose III attempted to erase her from history, possibly to secure the legitimacy of his own rule. Despite this effort, her accomplishments endure as a testament to her strength, intelligence, and ambition. She is remembered as a powerful and influential ruler who challenged traditional gender roles and left a lasting impact on Egyptian history and culture.
subtitle: The Female Pharaoh of Egypt
achievements: Maintained a period of peace and prosperity
Led successful military campaigns (early in reign)

enemies.name: Thutmose III (initially, later attempted to erase her)
Those who questioned her legitimacy as pharaoh

keyMyths.title: Divine Birth
keyMyths.description: Hatshepsut's divine birth was emphasized to legitimize her rule. It was claimed that Amun himself visited her mother, Ahmose, and conceived Hatshepsut, establishing her divine right to the throne.

keyMyths.title: Expedition to Punt
keyMyths.description: The expedition to the Land of Punt was a major achievement of Hatshepsut's reign. The expedition brought back valuable goods like frankincense, myrrh, ebony, ivory, and exotic animals, enriching Egypt and solidifying her power.

keyMyths.title: Transformation into a Male Pharaoh
keyMyths.description: To fully embody the role of pharaoh, Hatshepsut adopted male regalia and titles. She was often depicted with a beard and masculine physique in statues and artwork, further solidifying her authority and challenging traditional gender roles.

sources.author: Joyce Tyldesley
sources.description: A comprehensive biography of Hatshepsut, exploring her life, reign, and legacy.
sources.text: A comprehensive biography of Hatshepsut, exploring her life, reign, and legacy.

sources.author: Kara Cooney
sources.work: When Women Ruled the World: Six Queens of Egypt
sources.description: Examines the reigns of several female pharaohs, including Hatshepsut, and their impact on Egyptian history.
sources.text: Examines the reigns of several female pharaohs, including Hatshepsut, and their impact on Egyptian history.
